name: Octagon Verdict Data
defaultScope: Judge UFC accomplishments by default. Only mention the scope when it actually matters.

consumerGuidance.plainLanguageRule: Use total scores and category scores to decide the answer, but explain the answer with fan-friendly stats like title-fight wins, UFC record, elite wins, prime record, rounds-won percentage, active elite years, rivalry context, and loss context.
avoidInNormalAnswers: Raw category point totals like championship 11.68 vs 7.41
Footnotes or outside source citations unless asked

defaultFlow: Say if the comparison is close and who is ahead if useful.
Explain where Fighter A has the edge.
Explain where Fighter B has the edge.
Ask the right debate question for each side.
End with a clean final split.
avoid: Do not sound like a database or algorithm.
Do not use tables unless asked.

categoryDefinitions.championship: Title-level accomplishment, adjusted title wins, title-fight wins, and reign quality.
categoryDefinitions.opponentQuality: Quality and volume of elite wins.
categoryDefinitions.primeDominance: Prime record, round-winning, finish threat, separation, durability, and control.
categoryDefinitions.longevity: Active elite years and how long the fighter stayed relevant at the top level.
categoryDefinitions.penalty: Loss-context drag based on timing, opponent quality, finish context, and division context.

fighters.name: Jon Jones
fighters.oneLiner: The benchmark UFC resume: unmatched title-fight success, elite quality wins, long-term dominance, and no true competitive loss.
fighters.coreCase: Jones is the UFC benchmark: the deepest championship resume, elite quality wins, rare longevity, and no true competitive loss. He is the 99 OVR standard every other fighter is measured against.

humanStats.titleSummary: Normal 1.00. Interim 0.75. Vacant undisputed 0.90. Second division undisputed 1.25. Vacant second division 1.15. Total title fight wins = 16.
humanStats.lossContext: No contests excluded. Hamill DQ not treated as competitive dominance loss.

snapshot.Loss Context: No true competitive loss

strengths: The benchmark UFC resume: unmatched title-fight success, elite quality wins, long-term dominance, and no true competitive loss.
weaknesses: The main arguments against Jones are close fights, inactivity gaps, late-career sample size at heavyweight, and outside-the-cage controversy. But as a UFC resume, his in-cage case still has the strongest overall combination of title success, elite opponents, dominance, and longevity.
notes: No contests excluded. Hamill DQ not treated as competitive dominance loss.
Jones is the UFC benchmark: the deepest championship resume, elite quality wins, rare longevity, and no true competitive loss. He is the 99 OVR standard every other fighter is measured against.

fighters.name: Georges St-Pierre
fighters.oneLiner: The complete UFC resume: a legendary welterweight reign, elite quality wins, and one of the cleanest prime runs in the sport.
fighters.coreCase: St-Pierre is the complete champion case: elite title success, the best quality-wins score in this ranking, long-term consistency, and decisive answers to the biggest questions on his resume.

humanStats.primeRecord: 18-1 after first Hughes loss; Serra is the counted prime finish loss
humanStats.titleSummary: Normal 1.00. Interim Hughes 3 = 0.75. Bisping second-division undisputed title = 1.25. Total title fight wins = 13.
humanStats.lossContext: Hughes 2004 is an early elite loss. Serra 2007 is the counted prime finished loss, then avenged decisively.

snapshot.Loss Context: Hughes and Serra losses were avenged decisively

strengths: The complete UFC resume: a legendary welterweight reign, elite quality wins, and one of the cleanest prime runs in the sport.
weaknesses: Jon Jones still has the edge in championship volume and total time at the very top. St-Pierre's case is elite across the board, but the Serra upset and slightly lower title-fight total keep him just behind #1.
notes: Hughes 2004 is an early elite loss. Serra 2007 is the counted prime finished loss, then avenged decisively.
St-Pierre is the complete champion case: elite title success, the best quality-wins score in this ranking, long-term consistency, and decisive answers to the biggest questions on his resume.

fighters.name: Demetrious Johnson
fighters.oneLiner: The defining UFC flyweight champion: historic title control, elite technical dominance, and one of the cleanest prime skill sets in the sport.
fighters.coreCase: DJ is the flyweight standard: historic title control, elite technical completeness, and one of the cleanest dominance cases in the ranking.
fighters.bestArgument: DJ’s best argument is technical completeness plus title control: he was cleaner than Anderson, more dominant than most longer-resume fighters, and more complete than almost anyone skill-for-skill.

humanStats.titleSummary: DJ’s title case is historic at flyweight: long reign, repeated defenses, and clean separation from the division, with division-strength context applied.
humanStats.primeSummary: His UFC prime was long, technical, consistent, and rarely chaotic; he controlled fights without many ugly dips.
humanStats.lossContext: Cruz at bantamweight and Cejudo in a close flyweight title fight matter, but neither collapses the prime case.
humanStats.divisionStrengthContext: Flyweight dominance is fully respected, but opponent-quality value is division-adjusted.
humanStats.signatureWins: Benavidez, Dodson, Cejudo, Horiguchi, and years of flyweight title defenses give DJ a clean dominance case even after division-strength context.

snapshot.Loss Context: Cruz and Cejudo losses add context, not collapse

strengths: DJ wins when the debate rewards complete dominance, clean title control, and fewer resume holes over bigger-name but messier cases.
At his best, DJ was the most complete fighter in the sport: pace, wrestling, scrambling, submissions, striking, cardio, and fight IQ all working together.
His championship case is historically strong, even with division-strength context. The title reign was long, stable, and clearly separated him from the division.
The opponent list is good but division-adjusted. Benavidez, Dodson, Cejudo, Horiguchi, and others matter, but flyweight depth keeps the score below the very top names.
DJ’s longevity is strong because he stayed elite across a long title window without many ugly resume dents.
Flyweight context: his dominance is respected, while the division-strength adjustment keeps the quality-wins score below the very top tier.
Dominick Cruz loss: a real UFC loss at bantamweight, but not the core of his flyweight prime.
Henry Cejudo loss: matters because it ended the UFC reign, but it was close enough that it does not erase the championship run.
weaknesses: The only real drag is division depth. DJ’s skill and dominance are elite, but the flyweight opponent pool does not score like welterweight or lightweight.
DJ’s best counterargument is skill and cleanliness. He may not have the biggest names, but his technical control and consistency are almost impossible to ignore.

Johnson trails Jones and St-Pierre because his quality-wins score and flyweight division-strength context are lower in the current scoring model. His later non-UFC success adds historical context, but this ranking is based on the UFC resume.
notes: Prime starts with Dominick Cruz title fight and ends with Cejudo 2 title loss. Both are prime elite losses (-1.5 each). ONE Championship excluded.
Johnson is the UFC flyweight benchmark: historic title success, elite prime dominance, and a clean technical style that still grades near the top of the all-time list.

fighters.name: Anderson Silva
fighters.oneLiner: The peak-aura case: historic middleweight title control, terrifying finishing dominance, and one of the most iconic prime runs in UFC history.
fighters.coreCase: Anderson is the peak-aura legend: the most iconic middleweight reign, terrifying finishing ability, and some of the most memorable dominance moments ever.
fighters.bestArgument: Anderson’s best argument is peak aura: few fighters ever felt more dangerous, more creative, or more inevitable during a title reign.

humanStats.titleSummary: Anderson’s title case is a long middleweight reign built on repeated defenses, spectacular finishes, and one of the most iconic champion runs ever.
humanStats.primeSummary: His prime aura lasted for years, but the Weidman losses create drag because they land before the model fully shifts him into post-prime protection.
humanStats.lossContext: Weidman losses count as in-prime drag; later losses are mostly post-prime context.
humanStats.divisionStrengthContext: Middleweight dominance is central to the case, but opponent-quality value is slightly division-adjusted.
humanStats.signatureWins: Franklin, Henderson, Griffin, Belfort, Sonnen, Okami, and years of middleweight title wins give Anderson one of the sport’s most iconic reigns.

snapshot.Loss Context: Weidman losses count; later losses are treated as post-prime context

strengths: Anderson wins comparisons when peak impact, finishing danger, and iconic championship dominance outweigh cleaner but less explosive resumes.
At his best, Anderson felt untouchable. The counters, creativity, front kick, matrix defense, and sudden finishes gave his prime a mythology few fighters can match.
His championship case is huge: a long middleweight reign, repeated title wins, and a stretch where he felt like the most dangerous champion in the sport.
The opponent-quality case is strong but not perfect. The names matter, but middleweight division-strength context keeps this from being as clean as GSP or Jones.
Anderson’s elite window was long, but the back end is messy. This ranking protects later post-prime losses, while the Weidman losses still matter.
Peak aura: central to the Silva case and heavily reflected in the prime-dominance score.
Weidman losses: count as in-prime losses and are real resume drag.
Later losses: treated mostly as post-prime context rather than the core Silva case.
weaknesses: The Weidman losses matter because they land before this ranking fully moves Anderson into post-prime protection, and middleweight depth is slightly division-adjusted.
Anderson’s argument is aura and peak fear factor. If someone values the most terrifying prime, he has one of the strongest cases ever.

Silva does not pass the top three because the current scoring model gives Jones, St-Pierre, and Johnson stronger overall combinations of championship volume, opponent-quality wins, clean prime record, and loss context. The Weidman losses matter, and the middleweight division-strength adjustment keeps his quality-wins score below the very top tier.
notes: Ranking-based prime ends after Weidman rematch. Option A: both Weidman losses count as prime elite finished losses (-2.25 each); later losses are post-prime.

Silva is the UFC peak-aura legend: a historic champion, terrifying finisher, and one of the most influential dominant runs ever, with enough loss and opponent-strength context to keep him just behind the top three.

fighters.name: Islam Makhachev
fighters.oneLiner: The modern lightweight control case: elite finishing efficiency, high-end prime dominance, and a title run that keeps getting stronger.
fighters.coreCase: Islam is the modern lightweight resume case: elite skill, growing championship volume, and a deeper title-level resume than Khabib’s shorter run.
fighters.bestArgument: Islam’s best argument is modern depth: elite skill, growing title volume, quality wins across styles, and a prime run that is still strengthening.

humanStats.titleSummary: Islam’s title case has grown into a deeper modern lightweight reign, with more championship volume than a short peak-only case.
humanStats.primeSummary: His prime began later than Khabib’s scoring window but has already stacked elite years against modern lightweight and pound-for-pound level opponents.
humanStats.lossContext: The Martins loss is pre-prime and counts lightly; his prime scoring window starts around Drew Dober.
humanStats.divisionStrengthContext: Modern lightweight strength is a positive, and the later welterweight piece adds extra title-level value.
humanStats.signatureWins: Oliveira, Volkanovski, Poirier, Tsarukyan, and Hooker give Islam a deep modern lightweight ledger with real championship weight.

snapshot.Loss Context: Only UFC loss came pre-prime

strengths: Islam wins when the debate rewards total resume over pure peak. His championship volume and elite-win depth have become too much to ignore.
At his best, Islam is a complete control fighter with elite grappling, improved striking, patience, defense, and the ability to beat champions across styles.
His championship case is now the separator from Khabib. He has more title-fight volume and more time proving the belt against elite opponents.
The best wins carry serious weight: Oliveira, Volkanovski, Poirier, Tsarukyan, Hooker, and others give him a deep modern lightweight ledger.
Islam’s longevity is still building, but he has already stacked enough elite years to make the resume feel more complete than a short peak-only case.
Prime start: the main scoring window begins with Drew Dober in 2021.
Volkanovski wins: receive top-level quality-wins credit in this ranking.
Pre-prime loss: the Martins loss counts, but only lightly because it came before his prime.
weaknesses: The case is still building compared with older all-time resumes, and the pre-prime Martins loss remains a small drag.
The counterargument against Islam is that he still does not feel as untouchable as Khabib. The resume may be bigger, but the aura is not quite as clean.

He is still chasing the total volume of the fighters above him. The current scoring model also carries his pre-prime Martins loss and gives him fewer total elite-year reps than the older all-time resumes above him.
notes: Prime starts with Drew Dober 2021 contender-run start per user decision. Martins 2015 = pre-prime non-elite finished loss (-2.0). Volk wins corrected to max P4P champ opponent credit.
Islam is the modern lightweight benchmark: elite control, elite finishing, and a championship case that is already strong enough to sit in the all-time top five.

fighters.name: Khabib Nurmagomedov
fighters.oneLiner: The cleanest prime run at lightweight: unbeaten in the UFC, overwhelming round control, and the strongest dominance case in this ranking.
fighters.coreCase: Khabib is the cleanest peak-dominance case: unbeaten, overwhelming, and almost never in real trouble once he reached elite lightweight level.
fighters.bestArgument: Khabib’s best argument is purity: no UFC losses, no real collapse, and a final stretch that looked like total separation from elite lightweights.

humanStats.titleSummary: Khabib’s title resume is compact, but his defenses came against elite lightweights and he retired unbeaten on top.
humanStats.primeSummary: His prime run from RDA through Gaethje was one of the cleanest dominance stretches ever: short compared with long-reign champions, but almost flawless.
humanStats.lossContext: No UFC losses and no prime finish losses.
humanStats.divisionStrengthContext: Lightweight strength is a positive; his best work came in one of the toughest divisions.
humanStats.signatureWins: RDA, Barboza, McGregor, Poirier, and Gaethje give Khabib a compact but extremely high-quality lightweight resume.

snapshot.Loss Context: No UFC losses

strengths: Khabib wins when the debate rewards dominance, perfection, and how unbeatable a fighter looked at his best.
At his best, Khabib was the strongest control fighter in the sport. Everyone knew the game plan, and almost nobody had answers for it.
The championship case is strong but compact. He beat elite lightweights and retired on top, but he does not have the long title-fight volume of the highest-ranked champions.
The best wins are excellent: RDA, Barboza, McGregor, Poirier, and Gaethje give him a strong lightweight-quality case.
Khabib’s longevity is not weak, but it is not his main argument. His ranking is built more on peak dominance and perfect record than a massive title reign.
Prime dominance: the clearest strength of the Khabib case and the best score in this ranking.
No UFC losses: helps keep the resume unusually clean.
Lightweight strength: matters positively because his best work came in an elite division.
weaknesses: The drag is not quality of prime; it is volume. The title reign is shorter, the title-fight count is lower, and the resume has fewer layers than the long-reign GOAT cases.
Khabib’s argument against almost anyone is purity. No losses, no real collapse, no post-prime damage, and one of the clearest primes ever.

He does not climb higher because the current scoring model gives him less championship volume and fewer quality-wins layers than the fighters above him. His peak is elite enough to compete with anyone, but his total UFC resume is shorter.
notes: Prime starts with Rafael dos Anjos top-five breakthrough and ends with Gaethje title unification/retirement. Earlier UFC wins are pre-prime but no losses.
Khabib is the lightweight prime-dominance benchmark: unbeatable at his best, extremely efficient, and held back only by shorter championship volume than the names above him.

fighters.name: Alexander Volkanovski
fighters.oneLiner: The complete featherweight champion case: title consistency, strong quality wins, and one of the deepest modern resumes in the sport.
fighters.coreCase: Volk is the defining modern featherweight champion: elite title control, Max trilogy separation, Aldo win value, and a strong case across skill, resume, and era strength.
fighters.bestArgument: Volk’s best argument is balance plus direct separation: title success, elite wins, Max trilogy control, and modern featherweight strength.

humanStats.titleSummary: Volk’s title case is a clean modern featherweight reign built around Holloway trilogy separation and repeated contender defenses.
humanStats.primeSummary: His prime was not as long as Holloway’s total run, but at his best he was the defining featherweight of the era.
humanStats.lossContext: Islam losses receive reduced up-division elite-loss treatment; Topuria is the main prime-dominance drag.
humanStats.divisionStrengthContext: Modern featherweight strength helps his opponent-quality case.
humanStats.signatureWins: Aldo, Holloway three times, Ortega, Korean Zombie, and Yair give Volk one of the cleanest modern featherweight title resumes.

snapshot.Loss Context: Islam losses get reduced up-division treatment

strengths: Volk wins when head-to-head separation, championship control, and modern featherweight strength matter more than raw career volume.
At his best, Volk was adaptable, disciplined, fast, and difficult to solve. He could strike, wrestle, defend, adjust mid-fight, and win tactical battles against elite opponents.
His championship case is extremely clean at featherweight. The Max trilogy gives him direct separation over another all-time great from the same division.
Aldo, Holloway, Ortega, Korean Zombie, Yair, and Makhachev context give Volk a strong opponent-quality case, even with the Islam losses treated carefully.
Volk’s elite window was strong, but not as long as Holloway’s. His case is more about clean championship separation than pure career volume.
Jose Aldo win: marks the beginning of the prime scoring window.
Islam losses: receive reduced penalties because they were elite up-division title fights.
Topuria loss: counts as a meaningful main-division prime finished loss.
weaknesses: The Islam losses do not hurt as much because they were up-division elite fights, but the Topuria loss is real main-division drag and his prime-dominance score is not as high as the peak monsters.
Volk’s best argument is direct separation. He did not just share an era with Max; he beat him three times and became the defining featherweight champion.

The current scoring model hits him for the Topuria loss and keeps his prime-dominance score below the names with more overwhelming peaks. The up-division Islam losses are handled more lightly, but they still do not boost the resume the way a win would have.
notes: Prime starts with Jose Aldo. Islam losses treated with upward-division reduction; Topuria full main-division prime finished loss.
Volkanovski is the all-around featherweight standard: deep title work, strong quality wins, and a balanced resume with very few weak points.

fighters.name: Randy Couture
fighters.oneLiner: The old-school championship chaos case: heavyweight gold, light heavyweight gold, huge title moments, and a messy record built from constant risk.
fighters.coreCase: Couture is the chaos-era championship case: heavyweight gold, light heavyweight gold, huge title moments, and a resume that looks messy because he kept taking dangerous fights.

humanStats.titleSummary: Couture’s title case is built on winning UFC gold at heavyweight and light heavyweight, with repeated championship upsets and major title-fight moments.
humanStats.primeSummary: His best years were not one smooth prime; they were multiple veteran surges across divisions, which makes the case impressive but messy.
humanStats.lossContext: Losses are real drag, but many came in title or high-risk fights across divisions.
humanStats.divisionStrengthContext: Heavyweight and light heavyweight era context matter because his value comes from repeated title-level risk.
humanStats.signatureWins: Sylvia, Liddell, Ortiz, Belfort, Rizzo twice, Randleman, Gonzaga, and Coleman give Couture one of the most important old-school UFC win lists.

snapshot.Loss Context: Messy record, but constant title-risk context

strengths: Couture wins comparisons when championship moments, two-division relevance, and title-fight difficulty matter more than clean record aesthetics.
At his best, Couture was game-planning, clinch control, dirty boxing, wrestling pressure, and veteran nerve. He repeatedly solved bigger or more explosive champions.
The title case is the whole argument: multiple UFC championship runs, heavyweight and light heavyweight value, and major upset wins in early UFC history.
Sylvia, Liddell, Ortiz, Belfort, Rizzo, Randleman, Gonzaga, and Coleman give Couture a deep era-spanning UFC win list.
Couture’s longevity is unusual because he stayed relevant across weight classes and eras, but the case is also dragged down by losses and late-career damage.
Two-division value: heavyweight and light heavyweight gold are central to his ranking.
Era context: old-school volatility matters because he kept taking dangerous title fights.
Loss volume: keeps him below cleaner resumes even with strong championship credit.
weaknesses: The record is messy, and the loss penalty keeps him from the cleaner top-tier cases.
The case against Couture is the record. He has more losses than the cleaner GOAT candidates, so his ranking depends on how much credit you give for title risk and era context.

He does not climb higher because the loss penalty and prime-dominance profile are much messier than the cleaner GOAT cases. His greatness is championship value and risk-taking, not spotless dominance.
notes: Prime starts with Maurice Smith title win and runs through Nogueira top-five HW loss. Later Vera/Coleman/Toney/Machida stretch treated post-prime for penalties; active elite gaps capped at 18 months.
Couture is the championship-chaos legend: not clean, not smooth, but too decorated across UFC title history to rank like a normal flawed contender.

comparisonAngles: Two-Division Chaos Champion

Couture wins comparisons when championship moments, two-division relevance, and title-fight difficulty matter more than clean record aesthetics.

fighters.name: Max Holloway
fighters.oneLiner: The volume case: relentless pace, elite quality wins, and one of the longest useful elite windows in the featherweight era.
fighters.coreCase: Holloway is the volume king at featherweight: elite durability, huge opponent depth, long-term relevance, and one of the deepest bodies of work in the ranking.

humanStats.titleSummary: Holloway’s title case is strong, but his all-time case is even more about volume, elite wins, and relevance before and after the belt.
humanStats.primeSummary: Max’s prime and relevance window is unusually long: he stayed elite through multiple phases, even after losing the featherweight title.
humanStats.lossContext: Loss volume matters, but several losses came against elite opponents or across a long elite window.
humanStats.divisionStrengthContext: Featherweight value is strong, and the lightweight moments add context without becoming the core case.
humanStats.signatureWins: Aldo twice, Ortega, Kattar, Yair, Korean Zombie, and Gaethje give Holloway one of the strongest depth arguments in the ranking.

snapshot.Championship Level: Great but Not Massive Belt Volume
snapshot.Loss Context: Many losses came to elite names and across long volume

strengths: Holloway wins when the debate rewards longevity, opponent volume, durability, and sustained elite relevance.
At his best, Max was pace, boxing, durability, and pressure. He overwhelmed great fighters by turning fights into long, exhausting conversations they could not keep up with.
His championship case is strong, but not as clean as Volk’s because the trilogy directly separates them in the same era.
Holloway’s win depth is excellent: Aldo twice, Ortega, Kattar, Yair, Korean Zombie, Gaethje, and many more give him one of the strongest depth arguments in the ranking.
This is one of Max’s biggest edges. He has stayed elite for years, across multiple phases, even after losing the belt.
Quality wins: are the clearest strength of the Holloway case and rank #2 in this scoring model.
Longevity: is another major positive because he stayed elite for such a long period.
BMF belt: is not counted as UFC championship credit here.
weaknesses: The Volk trilogy blocks the clean featherweight supremacy argument, and the total loss count keeps him out of the top tier.
Max’s argument is total body of work. Even when someone peaked higher, Max often has more elite volume and more proof over time.

He sits below the very top names because the current scoring model gives him less championship control and more resume drag from total losses. The volume is impressive, but the belt dominance is not on the level of the names above him.
notes: Prime starts with Cub Swanson top-five breakthrough. BMF title wins are not counted as UFC championship title wins; BMF fights still count for quality wins/rounds. Loss context reaches current -10 cap.
Holloway is the volume-and-quality-wins monster of this ranking: one of the deepest win ledgers in the UFC, backed by real longevity, even without a top-tier championship score.

fighters.name: Kamaru Usman
fighters.oneLiner: The post-GSP welterweight champion case: dominant title control, elite round winning, and a focused but powerful championship peak.
fighters.coreCase: Usman is the great welterweight champion of the post-GSP era: dominant title run, elite round control, strong defenses, and one of the best championship peaks in the ranking.

humanStats.titleSummary: Usman’s title case is a strong modern welterweight reign with repeated defenses and real champion authority, even if the ending got damaged by Edwards.
humanStats.primeSummary: His prime was excellent but tighter than GSP’s or Holloway’s: a dominant title window rather than a decade-long all-time run.
humanStats.lossContext: The Edwards finish and rematch loss hurt the back end, but they came against elite opposition.

strengths: Penn wins comparisons when peak skill, lightweight title value, and the Hughes upset outweigh a messy career arc.

Hughes twice, Sherk, Florian, Sanchez, Stevenson, Pulver, and Uno give Penn real UFC value, with GSP and Hughes rivalry context shaping the case.

Late losses: do not erase the peak, but they drag the UFC-only resume hard.
weaknesses: The late-career collapse is the obvious drag and keeps the ranking from matching the peak-skill reputation.
Penn’s argument is peak skill and two-division greatness. The argument against him is the record collapse and too many damaging losses after the best years.
He does not rank higher because the late-career record collapse is too damaging, and the active elite window is not deep enough to offset the loss drag against cleaner champions.

Penn is a real two-division skill legend, but the UFC-only score has to balance the brilliant peak against one of the messiest late-career records in the ranking.

strengths: Cruz wins when the debate values tactical brilliance, comeback legacy, and peak skill over raw fight volume.

Dillashaw, Faber, Demetrious Johnson, and Mizugaki give Cruz meaningful UFC win value, though part of his broader historical case sits outside this scoring boundary.

Dillashaw comeback: a huge UFC legacy win and the center of his title case.

weaknesses: The injuries and WEC/UFC scoring boundary keep the résumé lighter than his full historical reputation.

Cruz is the injury-fragmented genius: one of the smartest and most unique champions ever, but UFC-only scoring keeps the total resume below the deeper title-volume cases.

Rashad stopped Chael late in Chael’s UFC run, treated as a post-prime/exit result.
Hendricks beat Robbie for the vacant belt, then Robbie won the rematch to become UFC welterweight champion.
Lawler beat Rory twice, including the legendary UFC 189 title defense that defines Robbie’s peak case.
Lawler officially defended against Condit in a razor-close title fight; the win counts with controversy context.
Woodley knocked out Lawler to win the welterweight title and end Robbie’s reign.
Diaz shocked young Robbie in their first UFC fight; Robbie won the late-career rematch.
Zhang went 2-0 against Joanna in UFC fights: one all-time classic title defense and one violent rematch finish. It is direct strawweight separation.
Rose beat Zhang twice in UFC strawweight title fights, including one finish. This is Rose’s strongest head-to-head GOAT argument and Zhang’s clearest ceiling.
Valentina beat Zhang in an upward-division flyweight title challenge. It matters, but carries reduced loss-context damage compared with a same-division title loss.
Zhang quickly stopped Andrade to take the strawweight title, capping Andrade’s reign.
Zhang submitted Esparza to take the title, ending Esparza’s second reign.
Rose went 2-0 against Joanna in UFC strawweight title fights: one title-winning finish and one five-round title defense.
Andrade knocked out Rose to win the title; Rose won the rematch. This is Andrade’s best title proof and Rose volatility context.
Esparza beat Rose twice in UFC title fights: first for the inaugural belt, then to regain the title years later.
Tate submitted Holm late to take the belt, capping Holm’s title reign at zero defenses.
Rousey submitted Tate in their UFC title fight. The broader Strikeforce rivalry is historically important, but only the UFC fight counts in this ranking.
Nunes submitted Tate at UFC 200 to take the bantamweight title, ending Tate’s prime championship window.
Dern went 2-0 against Jandiroba, including the vacant UFC strawweight title win. This is the backbone of Dern’s UFC-only title case.
Ribas beat Dern first, then Dern avenged the loss by submission. The split captures Dern’s growth and volatility.
Andrade finished Dern in a key contender fight, a strong late-prime Andrade win and Dern loss-context hit.
Kayla submitted Peña to win the title, ending Peña’s second reign.
Kayla submitted Holm in her UFC debut; post-prime Holm context but strong Kayla proof.
Harrison beat Vieira as the ranked contender bridge between her UFC debut and title win.

specialMatchups.winner: Valentina Shevchenko 1-1-1 edge by final title result
specialMatchups.summary: Grasso submitted Valentina to win the belt, retained in a draw, then lost the trilogy. This rivalry defines Grasso’s ceiling and Valentina’s later-career context.
Esparza beat Grasso before Grasso’s flyweight title rise.
Peña shocked Nunes to win the belt, then Nunes dominated the rematch. This rivalry defines Peña’s ceiling.
Valentina submitted Peña, a key elite-loss context point for Peña.
Holm knocked out Ronda to win the UFC bantamweight title, one of the biggest upset wins ever.
Valentina beat Joanna to win the UFC flyweight title. Joanna still owns the stronger strawweight-specific case, but Valentina owns the direct UFC championship result.
Nunes finished Rousey quickly in a UFC bantamweight title fight. It is a clean direct result and a symbolic passing-of-the-torch moment in women’s UFC history.
